Canada has become one of the first countries in the world to officially allow generative AI models to be trained on copyrighted materials, giving the country an advantage in the global race to develop the technology. The move comes as many countries tighten copyright rules to protect

rights holders in response to the emergence of AI systems capable of generating text, images and other media that can be difficult to distinguish from human-created works. According to the country's copyright office, Canadian law does not prohibit the use of copyrighted material in AI

training in all cases, provided the material is used solely for non-commercial purposes and does not harm the rights holder. The office has previously recommended that AI developers obtain permission from rights holders or rely on copyrighted material that is in the public domain or available under

a permissive license. It has not made any change to the policy in the ruling. However, the office said the new ruling "should reassure developers that the current approach is not a general prohibition on the use of copyrighted material in training AI models."<
